# Wide Band Imaging

Urvashi Rau, NRAO

21st NRAO Synthesis Imaging Workshop

29 May 2026

# Wide-Band terminology

|  Frequency Range | V_{min}, V_{max} | 1 - 2 GHz VLA L-Band | 8 - 12 GHz VLA X-Band  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Bandwidth | V_{max} - V_{min} | 1 GHz | 4 GHz  |
|  Bandwidth Ratio | V_{max} : V_{min} | 2 : 1 | 1.5 : 1  |
|  Fractional Bandwidth | (V_{max} - V_{min})/V_{mid} | 66 % | 40 %  |

# Wide-Band terminology

|  Frequency Range | V_{min}, V_{max} | 1 - 2 GHz VLA L-Band | 8 - 12 GHz VLA X-Band  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Bandwidth | V_{max} - V_{min} | 1 GHz | 4 GHz  |
|  Bandwidth Ratio | V_{max}: V_{min} | 2 : 1 | 1.5 : 1  |
|  Fractional Bandwidth | (V_{max} - V_{min})/V_{mid} | 66 % | 40 %  |

Continuum sensitivity improves with bandwidth.

$$
\sigma_{cont} = \frac{\sigma_{chan}}{\sqrt{(N_{chan})}} \propto \frac{T_{sys}}{\sqrt{N_{ant}(N_{ant} - 1) \delta \tau \delta \nu}}
$$

# Wide-Band terminology

|  Frequency Range | V_{min}, V_{max} | 1 - 2 GHz VLA L-Band | 8 - 12 GHz VLA X-Band  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Bandwidth | V_{max} - V_{min} | 1 GHz | 4 GHz  |
|  Bandwidth Ratio | V_{max} : V_{min} | 2 : 1 | 1.5 : 1  |
|  Fractional Bandwidth | (V_{max} - V_{min})/V_{mid} | 66 % | 40 %  |

Continuum sensitivity improves with bandwidth.

$$
\sigma_{cont} = \frac{\sigma_{chan}}{\sqrt{(N_{chan})}} \propto \frac{T_{sys}}{\sqrt{N_{ant}(N_{ant} - 1) \delta \tau \delta \nu}}
$$

Imaging complexity due to frequency dependence of the sky and instrument increases with bandwidth ratio

# Sky and Instrument are frequency-dependent

$$
V_{ij}^{obs}(\mathbf{v},t) = M_{ij}(\mathbf{v},t)S_{ij}(\mathbf{v},t)\iiint M_{ij}^{dd}(l,m,\mathbf{v},t)I(l,m,\mathbf{v},t)e^{2\pi i(ul + vm + w(n - 1))}dl\,dm\,dn
$$

UV coverage

Primary Beam

Sky Brightness

$$
V_1 < V_2
$$

# Multi Frequency UV-coverage ( and resolution )

Angular resolution changes with frequency.

Observed image:  $I_{\mathrm{v}}^{obs} = I_{\mathrm{v}}^{sky} * PSF_{\mathrm{v}}$

# Multi Frequency UV-coverage ( and resolution )

Original resolution image:  $I_{\mathrm{v}}^{obs} = I_{\mathrm{v}}^{sky} * PSF_{\mathrm{v}}$

Angular resolution changes with frequency.

## Method 1: Cube

- Image each channel
- Smooth all to match lowest resolution
- Add them together

Simplest technique, but limited angular resolution

# Cube Imaging + Image sum

## Method 1: Cube

- Image each channel
- Smooth to match lowest resolution
- Add them together

- Lower noise
- Lower resolution

1.0 GHz

1.2 GHz

1.4 GHz

1.6 GHz

1.8 GHz

# Multi Frequency Synthesis

Observed image:  $I_{\mathrm{v}}^{obs} = I_{\mathrm{v}}^{sky} * PSF_{\mathrm{v}}$

$I_{wb}^{obs} = \sum_{\nu}\left[I_{\nu}^{sky}*PSF_{\nu}\right]$

# Multi Frequency Synthesis

Angular resolution and PSF sidelobe levels are defined by the combined UV-coverage.

## Method 2: WideBand

- Image and deconvolve all data together

Flat-spectrum Source ⇒ All OK.

Steep-spectrum source
⇒ Need a wide band sky model

$$
I _ {wb} ^ {obs} = \sum_ {v} \left[ I _ {v} ^ {sky} * PSF _ {v} \right]
$$

# Multi Frequency Synthesis

Angular resolution and PSF sidelobe levels are defined by the combined UV-coverage.

## Method 2: WideBand

- Image and deconvolve all data together

Flat-spectrum Source ⇒ All OK.

Steep-spectrum source
⇒ Need a wide band sky model

# Multi Frequency Synthesis

Angular resolution and PSF sidelobe levels are defined by the combined UV-coverage.

## Method 2: WideBand

- Image and deconvolve all data together

Flat-spectrum Source → All OK.

Steep-spectrum source
→ Need a wide band sky model

# Which sources need wideband modeling?

- Steep Spectrum source
- Low noise level

Artifacts are visible above the noise.

=> Need a wideband model (nterms>1)

- Steep Spectrum source
- High noise level

Artifacts are below the noise level

=> Flat-spectrum model will suffice

Images from J.Marvil
Algorithms that make a wide band sky model

# Algorithms that make a wide band sky model

Solve for a sky model independently per channel

## Intensity model

- Delta-functions,
Gaussians,
Paraboloids, etc as 'atoms'

(Combine channels or fit a spectrum only with the output image cube)

# Algorithms that make a wide band sky model

## Intensity model

- Delta-fns, Gaussians, Paraboloids, etc as 'atoms'

## Spectral model (per 'atom')

- Stokes I : Polynomials, Power Laws
- Polarization : Faraday depth structure

# Algorithms that make a wide band sky model

Multi-term MFS deconvolution (splemmode='mfs' or 'mvc' in CASA tclean)

Solve for spectral Taylor polynomial coefficients  $I_{\nu}^{sky} = \sum_{t} I_{t}^{m}\left(\frac{\nu - \nu_{0}}{\nu_{0}}\right)^{t}$  (Multi-term linear least squares)

Interpret coefficients as a power-law (spectral index and curvature)

$$
I _ {v} = I _ {v _ {0}} \left(\frac{v}{v _ {0}}\right) ^ {\alpha + \beta \log (v / v _ {0})} \quad \rightarrow \quad I _ {0} ^ {m} = I _ {v _ {0}} \quad \quad I _ {1} ^ {m} = I _ {v _ {0}} \alpha \quad \quad I _ {2} ^ {m} = I _ {v _ {0}} \left(\frac{\alpha (\alpha - 1)}{2} + \beta\right)
$$

Rau & Cornwell, 2011

Sault & Wieringa, 1994

WS-Clean (Offringa/Smirnov 2017) & Obit MF-Image (Cotton, 2025):

- Other Taylor-term and Cube-based modeling options during the deconvolution step.
- MF-Image also chooses the order of the polynomial, based on the source SNR

# High Dynamic Range Continuum Imaging - 3C286 VLA

NTERMS = 1

Rms: 9 mJy -- 1 mJy

DR: 1600 - 13000

NTERMS = 2

Rms: 1 mJy -- 0.2 mJy

DR: 10,000 - 17,000

NTERMS = 3

Rms: 0.2 mJy -- 85 uJy

DR: 65,000 - 170,000

NTERMS = 4

Rms 0.14 mJy -- 80 uJy

DR: >110,000 - 180,000

# Example : Abell 2256 ( Owen et al, 2014 )

Intensity

Intensity weighted Spectral Index

VLA A,B,C,D at L-Band (1-2 GHz), VLA A at S&C bands(2-4, 4-6, 6-8 GHz)
# Wide Band Primary Beams

# Wide Band Primary Beams

Spectral Index of PB (About -1.4 at the HPBW)

$$
I _ {w f, w b} ^ {obs} = \sum_ {\nu} \left[ \left(P _ {\nu} \cdot I _ {\nu} ^ {sky}\right) * PSF _ {\nu} \right]
$$

Single Pointing Image has an artificial PB spectral index away from the pointing center

# Handling Wide-Band Primary Beams during imaging

Implemented in CASA tclean as specmode='mvc'

Output Sky model

Intensity: Sky x PB_avg
Spectrum: Sky only

Other options (limited accuracy):

- Apply WB-PB-cor during gridding for MFS imaging (specmode='mfs')
- Do 'widebandpbcor' as a post-deconvolution step only (gridder='standard')

# Example : Spectral index in G55 supernova remnant

Without WB PB correction

With WB PB correction

# Wide Band Mosaic Primary Beam

The mosaic primary beam has an artificial spectral index all over the combined F-O-V

=> Need wide-band PB correction prior to wideband sky modeling and joint deconvolution. (Implemented in CASA as specmode='mvc' with gridder='mosaic' or 'awp2')

# Example : Galactic Center ( SARAO, I. Heywood et al 2002, J.C. Munoz-Mateos )

Wideband Model per pointing
Linear Mosaic
Include single-dish data
Dir-dep Cal
Using MeerKAT's dense UV-coverage
WS-Clean, etc.

# Accuracy of wide-band models

How accurately can we reconstruct sky spectra, at different spatial scales?

- Point sources
- Extended emission
- Very large scales (spatial scales comparable to the UV hole)

Consider the signature of broadband emission, on the UV-plane.
Range of Spatial Scales
Range of Spatial Scales
Range of Spatial Scales
Range of Spatial Scales

# Point Sources – Cube vs WideBand

For most compact sources

Can model the structure and spectrum at high resolution

Restored Intensity image

Spectral Index map

# Extended Emission - Multi-scale spectra

Intensity Image

Average Spectral Index

Gradient in Spectral Index

Accuracy depends on how good the multi-scale emission model is.

Compare two options :

(1) Multi-scale source model
(1) Delta-fn source model

For both : Use a polynomial spectrum per flux component

# Extended Emission - Multi-scale spectra

Intensity Image

Average Spectral Index

Gradient in Spectral Index

MT-MFS

# Very Large Spatial Scales – Unconstrained spectrum

Example : Flat spectrum emission at large scales

With only interferometer data

=> Wideband modeling can get the spectrum wrong.

=> Add wideband single dish data

# Very Large Spatial Scales – Constrain with single-dish data

Example : Flat spectrum emission at large scales

With only interferometer data

$\Rightarrow$ Wideband modeling can get the spectrum wrong.

$\Rightarrow$ With SD data, better reconstruction of intensity and spectrum at very large scales

Algorithms

- tp2vis (Koda et al, 2011)
- SDInt (Rau, Naik, Braun 2018)

# Other related topics

(1) Wide Band Self Calibration & Continuum Subtraction

(1) Modeling sky polarization effects (e.g. Faraday rotation measure)

(1) Bandwidth smearing limits

- Relevant when choosing ' how much to average your data ' to control data volumes

# Other uses of Wide Band Sky models

## Wide Band Self-Calibration

- Eg: To fix calibration errors across spws

# Other uses of Wide Band Sky models

## Wide Band Self-Calibration

- Eg: To fix calibration errors across spws

## Continuum Subtraction

- To fit and remove continuum spectrum from underneath spectral lines of interest

# Wide Band Polarization

## Stokes Q,U,V can also change with frequency

- If the expected variation < ~1% of the peak, MFS (nt=1) will suffice
- If not, it is safest to make a Cube (as the spectra may not smooth)

## Faraday Rotation-Measure Synthesis
Brentjens, 2008, Bell et al, 2013

Images of polarized surface-brightness at various Faraday-depths : $F(\varphi)$

- $P = Q + iU$ : Make spectral cubes for Q and U separately, and calculate P
- For each pixel in the P-cube, solve $P(\lambda^2) = \int F(\varphi)e^{2\pi i\varphi \chi^2}d\varphi$ for $F(\varphi)$

# Bandwidth smearing

Excessive channel averaging of visibilities will cause radial smearing

Radial shift of visibilities on the UV plane + Similarity theorem of Fourier transforms

Bandwidth smearing limit for HPBW f-o-v =  $\delta_{\mathrm{V}} < \frac{v_0D}{b_{\max}}$  (chan avg limit)

1 MHz for VLA A, 30 MHz for VLA D

# Imaging with frequency-dependent Sky + Instrument

$$
V _ {ij} ^ {obs} (\mathbf{v}, t) = M _ {ij} (\mathbf{v}, t) S _ {ij} (\mathbf{v}, t) \iiint_ {\downarrow} M _ {ij} ^ {dd} (l, m, \mathbf{v}, t) I (l, m, \mathbf{v}, t) e ^ {2 \pi i (u l + v m + w (n - 1))} d l d m d n
$$
